elements.sent-2411-001: The Wannier-Stark ladder (WSL) is a basic concept, supporting periodic oscillation, widely used in many areas of physics.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we investigate the formations of WSL in generalized systems, including strongly correlated and non-Hermitian systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: We present a theorem on the existence of WSL for a set of general systems that are translationally symmetric before the addition of a linear potential.
elements.sent-2411-004: For a non-Hermitian system, the WSL becomes complex but maintains a real energy level spacing.
elements.sent-2411-005: We illustrate the theorem using 1D extended Bose-Hubbard models with both real and imaginary hopping strengths.
elements.sent-2411-006: It is shown that the Bloch-Zener oscillations of correlated bosons are particularly remarkable under resonant conditions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Numerical simulations for cases with boson numbers $n=2$, $3$, and $4$ are presented.
elements.sent-2411-008: Analytical and numerical results for the time evolution of the $n$ -boson-occupied initial state indicate that all evolved states exhibit quasi periodic oscillations, but with different profiles, depending on the Hermiticity and interaction strength.
elements.sent-2411-009: Rigorous results in quantum many-body systems are rare but crucial for gaining valuable insights into the characterization of dynamic behaviors within correlated systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: For non-interacting system, Wannier-Stark (WS) ladders are a theoretical model in solid-state physics that describe the quantization of electronic states in a crystal under the influence of a constant electric field \cite {Bloch1929,wannier1959elements,Wannier1960,Glueck2002,Waschke1993 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This has been observed in systems of semiconductor superlattices and ultracold atoms \cite{Waschke1993,BenDahan1996,Wilkinson1996,Anderson1998 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It is closely related to Bloch oscillations (BOs) due to the equidistant spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-013: In the past decade, such a phenomenon has attracted much attention in cold-atoms physics and photonics due to applications in interferometric measurements and as a method for manipulating localized wave packets \cite {Breid2006,Breid2007,Dreisow2009,Kling2010,Ploetz2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: It can be simulated using artificial quantum systems, such as superconducting circuits \cite {Song2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: On the other hand, the dynamics of particle pairs in lattice systems have garnered considerable interest, owing to the rapid advancements in experimental techniques.
elements.sent-2411-016: Ultra-cold atoms have proven to be an ideal testing ground for few-particle fundamental physics, as optical lattices offer clean realizations of a variety of many-body Hamiltonians.
elements.sent-2411-017: It stimulates many experimental \cite{Winkler2006,Foelling2007,Gustavsson2008 and theoretical investigations \cite {Mahajan2006,Petrosyan2007,Creffield2007,Kuklov2007,Zoellner2008,Wang2008,Valiente2008,Jin2009,Valiente2009,Valiente2010,Javanainen2010,Wang2010,Rosch2008,Zhang2024 in strongly correlated systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recently, it has been shown that a bound pair within a correlated system also exhibits periodic dynamics when subjected to a linear potential \cite {Khomeriki2010,Longhi2011,Longhi2012,Corrielli2013,Lin2014,ZhangXZ2016,Zhang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work, we extend the concept of WSL to the many-body systems.
elements.sent-2411-020: We investigate the formations of WSL in generalized systems, including strongly correlated and non-Hermitian systems.
elements.sent-2411-021: We show that the WSL becomes complex but maintains a real energy level spacing for a non-Hermitian system.
elements.sent-2411-022: To illustrate the theorem, we focus on 1D extended Bose-Hubbard models with both real and imaginary hopping strengths.
elements.sent-2411-023: Numerical simulations for cases with boson numbers $n=2$ , $3$, and $4$ are presented.
elements.sent-2411-024: Analytical and numerical results for the time evolution of the $n$-boson-occupied initial state indicate that all evolved states exhibit quasi periodic oscillations, but with different profiles, depending on the Hermiticity and interaction strength.
elements.sent-2411-025: This paper is organized as follows.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-026: Finally, we provide a summary in Sec.. \ref{Summary .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In general, a linear field can break the translational symmetry and disrupt the energy band structure of a non-interacting system.
elements.sent-2411-028: For some simple systems, it has been precisely demonstrated that the formation of WSLs leads to periodic dynamics, specifically Bloch oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, obtaining the energy levels for complex systems, particularly those with interactions, is quite challenging.
elements.sent-2411-030: The main aim of this paper is to determine whether WSLs still exist in general systems.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this section, we present a theorem on the structure of energy levels for general systems.
elements.sent-2411-032: We focus solely on the 1D system for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-033: The obtained conclusion is applicable to higher-dimensional systems.
elements.sent-2411-034: We start with a general tight-binding model with the Hamiltonian in the form \begin{equation}H=H_{0}+\omega\sum_{j=-\infty }^{\infty }\sum_{\sigma =1}^{\Lambda }ja_{j,\sigma }^{\dag }a_{j,\sigma },\end{equation} where $a_{j,\sigma ^{\dag }$\ ($a_{j,\sigma }$) is the boson or fermion creation (annihilation) operator, with internal degree of freedom (or flavor) $\sigma =\left[ 1,\Lambda \right] $, at the $i$th site.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Theorem.---The energy levels of Hamiltonian $H$ must consist of multi-set of WSLs with an identical real level spacing, which is independent of $H_{0}$, if $H_{0}$ is constructed under the following conditions: (i) The total particle number $\sum_{j=-\infty ^{\infty }\sum_{\sigma =1 ^{\Lambda a_{j,\sigma ^{\dag a_{j,\sigma }$\ is conservative, that is \begin{equation}\lbrack \sum_{j=-\infty }^{\infty }\sum_{\sigma =1}^{\Lambda }a_{j,\sigma }^{\dag }a_{j,\sigma },H_{0}]=0.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Proof.--Based on the assumptions on $H$, we simply have \begin{equation}\left[ T_{r_{0}},H\right] =-r_{0}\omega\sum_{j=-\infty }^{\infty }\sum_{\sigma =1}^{\Lambda }a_{j,\sigma }^{\dag }a_{j,\sigma },\end{equation} which is referred to as a ramped translational symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then we always have \begin{equation}H\left( T_{r_{0}}|\psi _{0}\rangle \right) =\left( E_{0}+r_{0}\mathcal{\omega }\right) \left( T_{r_{0}}|\psi _{0}\rangle \right) ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}H\left( T_{r_{0}}^{-1}|\psi _{0}\rangle \right) =\left( E_{0}-r_{0}\mathcal{ \omega}\right) \left( T_{r_{0}}|\psi _{0}\rangle \right) ,\end{equation} i.e., $T_{r_{0 |\psi _{0}\rangle $ ($T_{r_{0 ^{-1 |\psi _{0}\rangle $)\ is also the eigenstate of $H$\ with eigen energy $E_{0 +r_{0}\mathcal{\omega}$ ( $E_{0 -r_{0}\mathcal{\omega}$).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Operator $T_{r_{0 }$\ increments the energy level by a step of $r_{0}\mathcal{\omega}$ and the operator $T_{r_{0 ^{-1}$ decrements the energy level by a step of $r_{0}\mathcal{\omega}$.\ We can then construct a set of eigenstates \begin{equation}|\psi _{m}\rangle =\left( T_{r_{0}}\right) ^{m}|\psi _{0}\rangle ,\end{equation} $(m=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,...)$ with eigenenergy \begin{equation}E_{m}=E_{0}+mr_{0}\mathcal{\omega}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: It indicates that the translational operator $T_{r_{0 }$\ acts as a ladder operator.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We note that based on another eigenstate $|\psi _{0 ^{\prime }\rangle $, which does not be long to $\left\{ |\psi _{m}\rangle \right\} $, another set of eigenstates $\left\{ |\psi _{m ^{\prime }\rangle \right\} $ can be generated accordingly.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Although $E_{0}$\ can be real or complex, dependenting on the detail of the Hamiltonian $H_{0}$, we can conclude that the energy levels of Hamiltonian $ H $ consists of multi-set of WSLs with an identical real level spacing $r_{0 \mathcal{\omega}$.}

elements.sent-2411-044: This rigorous conclusion has important implications for investigating the dynamics of many-body and non-Hermitian systems in the presence of a linear field.
elements.sent-2411-045: In the following sections, we will focus on both the Hermitian and non-Hermitian extended Bose-Hubbard models to demonstrate the application of our conclusions and to reveal the dynamics of correlated bosons.
elements.sent-2411-046: We consider an extended boson Hubbard model describing interacting particles in the lowest Bloch band of a one dimensional lattice, which can be employed to describe ultracold atoms or molecules with magnetic or electric dipole-dipole interactions in optical lattices.
elements.sent-2411-047: We focus on the dynamics of the bosonic cluster, which is $n$ identical bosons in a bound state.
elements.sent-2411-048: For the simplest case with $n=2$, it has been demonstrated that, as another type of bound pair, it allows for correlated single-particle tunneling, as shown in previous work \cite{Jin2011,Lin2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Such a bound pair can act as a quasi-particle, with an energy band width of the same order as that of a single particle.
elements.sent-2411-050: One of the objectives of this paper is to demonstrate that this type of bound state can be generalized to an $n$-boson system.
elements.sent-2411-051: We consider an illstrative example that $H_{0}$\ is a one-dimensional extended Bose-Hubbard model with the Hamiltonian \begin{eqnarray}H_{\text{B}} = H_{0}+\omega \sum_{j=-\infty }^{\infty }jn_{j}, \notag H_{0} = -\kappa \sum_{j=1}\left( b_{j}^{\dagger }b_{j+1}+\text{ H.c .}\right) \notag +\sum_{j=-\infty }^{\infty }[\frac{U}{2}n_{j}\left( n_{j}-1\right) +Vn_{j}n_{j+1}], \label{H_B}\end{eqnarray} where $b_{j ^{\dag }$ and $n_{j =b_{j ^{\dag b_{j}$\ is the creation and number operators of the boson at the $j$th site.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Parameters $\kappa $, $U$\ and $V$,\ denote the tunneling strength, on-site and NN interactions between bosons.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this work, we consider the cases with real and imaginary , respectively.

elements.sent-2411-058: The other is an anti-linear operator $\mathcal{T}$, which is the time-reversal operator defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal{T}\sqrt{-1}\mathcal{T}^{-1}=-\sqrt{-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: We note that the extended Bose-Hubbard $H_{\text{B }$ has $\mathcal{ PT}$ symmetry, that is \begin{equation}\mathcal{PT}H_{\text{B}}\mathcal{(PT)}^{-1}=H_{\text{B}},\end{equation} when we consider the imaginary hopping strength, $\kappa =i\left\vert \kappa \right\vert $.}
elements.sent-2411-060: According to non-Hermitian quantum mechanics \cite {Scholtz1992 , the Hamiltonian $H_{\text{B }$ is pseudo-Hermitian, in which complex energy levels appear in the conjugate pairs.}}

elements.sent-2411-063: Let us begin by analyzing in detail the many-boson problem within the Hamiltonian $H_{0}$ with zero $ \kappa $.
elements.sent-2411-064: It is clear that, in the invariant subspace with fixed boson number $n$, there is a set of degenerate eigenstates $\left\{ \left\vert l\right\rangle ,l\in \left( -n\infty ,n\infty \right) \right\} $ of $ H_{0}\left( \kappa =0\right) $ with eigen energy $\varepsilon (n)$ $ =Un(n-1)/2$, which are expressed as the form \begin{equation}\left\vert jn+\lambda \right\rangle =\frac{\left( b_{j}^{\dagger }\right) ^{n-\lambda }\left( b_{j+1}^{\dagger }\right) ^{\lambda}}{\sqrt{\left( n+1-\lambda \right) !\left( \lambda -1\right) !}}|\mathrm{vac}\rangle ,\end{equation} with $j\in \left( -\infty ,\infty \right) $\ and $\lambda \in \lbrack 0,n-1]$ .
elements.sent-2411-065: Here $\left\vert \mathrm{vac}\right\rangle $\ is the vacuum state for the boson operator $b_{j}$.
elements.sent-2411-066: It is easy to check that the nearest energy level next to $\varepsilon (n)$\ is $\varepsilon (n)-U$, resulting in an energy gap of $U$.\ This ensures us to perform the first-order perturbation approximation when the hopping term\ is considered under the strong correlation condition where $U\gg \left\vert \kappa \right\vert $.
elements.sent-2411-067: In order to obtain the effective Hamiltonian, one can simply envalue the nonzero matrix elements of $H_{\text{B }$\ in the subspace spanned by the set of eigenstates $\left\{ \left\vert l\right\rangle \right\} $.}
elements.sent-2411-068: For a given boson number $n$, the effective Hamiltonian can be written as \begin{eqnarray}H_{\mathrm{eff}}^{[n]} = -\kappa \sum_{j,\lambda }h_{j,\lambda }+U\frac{ n(n-1)}{2}\sum_{l=-\infty }^{\infty }\left\vert l\right\rangle \left\langle l\right\vert \notag +\omega \sum_{l=-\infty }^{\infty }l\left\vert l\right\rangle \left\langle l\right\vert ,\end{eqnarray} where \begin{eqnarray}h_{j,\lambda } = \sqrt{(n-\lambda )(\lambda +1)}\left\vert jn+\lambda \right\rangle \notag \times \left\langle jn+\lambda +1\right\vert +\text{ H.c.}.\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-069: In its present form, $H_{\mathrm{eff ^{[n]}$ are formally analogous to a tight-binding model describing a single-particle dynamics in an infinite chain with NN hopping, under a linear field.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We note that the states $ \left\vert l\right\rangle $\ in each unit cell represent an $n$ -bosonic-cluster state, in which all $n$ bosons occupy a single dimer of the original lattice of the Bose-Hubbard system $H_{\text{B }$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this sense, the dynamics of $H_{\mathrm{eff ^{[n]}$ is essentially\ the dynamics of $n$ correlated bosons.}}

elements.sent-2411-123: In summary, we have developed a theory for a class of Hermitian and non-Hermitian Hamiltonians that possess WSLs.
elements.sent-2411-124: Such Hamiltonians consist of two distinct parts: an arbitrary Hamiltonian exhibiting translational symmetry and a linear potential.
elements.sent-2411-125: The most fascinating and important feature of these systems is that their conclusions are independent of the details of the Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-126: As examples, we have investigate the extended Bose-Hubbard model with several bosons.
elements.sent-2411-127: Analytic and numerical approaches verified our conclusion in the case with $U=V$.
elements.sent-2411-128: We also demonstrated the application of the theorem by analyzing dynamics driven by the Hubbard model using numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-129: Our findings provide a method for analyzing the energy-level structure of general systems and are expected to be both necessary and insightful for quantum engineering in many-body systems.
elements.sent-2411-130: Numerical simulations for cases with boson numbers $N=2$, $3$, and $4$ are presented.
elements.sent-2411-131: Analytical and numerical results for the time evolution of the $N$ -boson-occupied initial state indicate that all evolved states exhibit quasi periodic oscillations, but with different profiles, depending on the Hermiticity and interaction strength.
elements.sent-2411-132: Rigorous results in quantum { interacting systems are rare but crucial for gaining valuable insights into the characterization of dynamic behaviors within correlated systems.}
elements.sent-2411-133: For non-interacting system, Wannier-Stark ladders (WSL) are a theoretical model in solid-state physics that describe the quantization of electronic states in a crystal under the influence of a constant electric field \cite {Bloch1929,wannier1959elements,Wannier1960,Glueck2002,Waschke1993 .}
elements.sent-2411-134: Ultra-cold atoms have proven to be an ideal testing ground for few-particle fundamental physics, as optical lattices offer clean realizations of a variety of { interacting Hamiltonian systems .}
elements.sent-2411-135: It stimulates many experimental \cite {Winkler2006,Foelling2007,Gustavsson2008 and theoretical investigations \cite {Mahajan2006,Petrosyan2007,Creffield2007,Kuklov2007,Zoellner2008,Wang2008,Valiente2008,Jin2009,Valiente2009,Valiente2010,Javanainen2010,Wang2010,Rosch2008,Zhang2024 in strongly correlated systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: In this work, we extend the concept of WSL to the { interacting Hamiltonian systems .}

elements.sent-2411-141: Operator $T_{r}$\ increments the energy level by a step of $Nr\mathcal{\omega }$ and the operator $T_{r ^{-1}$ decrements the energy level by a step of $Nr\mathcal{\omega }$.\ We can then construct a set of eigenstates \begin{equation}|\psi _{m}\rangle =\left( T_{r}\right) ^{m}|\psi _{0}\rangle ,\end{equation} $(m=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,...)$ with eigenenergy \begin{equation}E_{m}=E_{0}+mNr\mathcal{\omega }.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-142: It indicates that the translational operator $T_{r}$\ acts as a ladder operator.
elements.sent-2411-143: Although $E_{0}$\ can be real or complex, dependenting on the detail of the Hamiltonian $H_{0}$, we can conclude that the energy levels of Hamiltonian $ H $ consists of multi-set of WSLs with an identical real level spacing $Nr \mathcal{\omega}$.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, a large energy level spacing that exceeds the scale of low-energy physics is meaningless.
elements.sent-2411-145: Here, we provide an example to demonstrate this point.
elements.sent-2411-146: Consider a system with a set of $n$-boson cluster eigenstates in the $n$ -boson invariant subspace.
elements.sent-2411-147: These eigenstates are connected by the translational operator.
elements.sent-2411-148: In this situation, one could easily have a large number $K$ of widely separated $n$-boson clusters to construct the eigenstates with a total boson number $N=Kn$.
elements.sent-2411-149: The WSL frequency spacing derived above for states of that multi-cluster kind would therefore be $Knr\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-150: However, there exist many subsets of eigenstates, which can be constructed by translating one $n$-boson cluster while leaving the other $K-1$ clusters unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-151: Then, the corresponding energy levels in a subset form the WSL with frequency spacing $nr\omega$ rather than $Nr\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-152: This indicates that a system with a large boson number $N=Kn$ can also support dynamics with a smaller frequency $nr\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-153: This rigorous conclusion has important implications for investigating the dynamics of { interacting and non-Hermitian systems in the presence of a linear field.}
elements.sent-2411-154: We focus on the dynamics of the bosonic cluster, which is $N$ identical bosons in a bound state.

elements.sent-2411-156: One of the objectives of this paper is to demonstrate that this type of bound state can be generalized to an $N$-boson system.

elements.sent-2411-158: It is clear that, in the invariant subspace with fixed boson number $N$, there is a set of degenerate eigenstates $\left\{ \left\vert l\right\rangle ,l\in \left( -N\infty ,N\infty \right) \right\} $ of $ H_{0}\left( \kappa =0\right) $ with eigen energy $\varepsilon (N)$ $ =UN(N-1)/2$, which are expressed as the form \begin{equation}\left\vert jN+\lambda \right\rangle =\frac{\left( b_{j}^{\dagger }\right) ^{N-\lambda }\left( b_{j+1}^{\dagger }\right) ^{\lambda}}{\sqrt{\left( N-\lambda \right) !\left( \lambda \right) !}}|\mathrm{vac}\rangle ,\end{equation} with $j\in \left( -\infty ,\infty \right) $\ and $\lambda \in \lbrack 0,N-1]$ .
elements.sent-2411-159: It is easy to check that the nearest energy level next to $\varepsilon (N)$\ is $\varepsilon (N)-U$, resulting in an energy gap of $U$.\ This ensures us to perform the first-order perturbation approximation when the hopping term\ is considered under the strong correlation condition where $U\gg \left\vert \kappa \right\vert $.
elements.sent-2411-160: In order to obtain the effective Hamiltonian, one can simply evaluate the nonzero matrix elements of $H_{\text{B }$\ in the subspace spanned by the set of eigenstates $\left\{ \left\vert l\right\rangle \right\} $.}
elements.sent-2411-161: For a given boson number $N$, the effective Hamiltonian can be written as \begin{eqnarray}H_{\mathrm{eff}}^{[N]} = -\kappa \sum_{j,\lambda }h_{j,\lambda }+U\frac{ N(N-1)}{2}\sum_{l=-\infty }^{\infty }\left\vert l\right\rangle \left\langle l\right\vert \notag +\omega \sum_{l=-\infty }^{\infty }l\left\vert l\right\rangle \left\langle l\right\vert , \label{H_eff}\end{eqnarray} where \begin{eqnarray}h_{j,\lambda } = \sqrt{(N-\lambda )(\lambda +1)}\left\vert jN+\lambda \right\rangle \notag \times \left\langle jN+\lambda +1\right\vert +\text{ H.c.}\end{eqnarray} In its present form, $H_{\mathrm{eff ^{[N]}$ are formally analogous to a tight-binding model describing a single-particle dynamics in an infinite chain with NN hopping, under a linear field.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: We note that the states $ \left\vert l\right\rangle $\ in each unit cell represent an $N$ -bosonic-cluster state, in which all $N$ bosons occupy a single dimer of the original lattice of the Bose-Hubbard system $H_{\text{B }$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: In this sense, the dynamics of $H_{\mathrm{eff ^{[N]}$ is essentially\ the dynamics of $N$ correlated bosons.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: Notably, {we would like to point out that the hopping strength in }$H_{ \mathrm{eff ^{[N]}${\ is of the order of }$\kappa ${, not }$\kappa ^{2 /U${ .}}}}

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{slinky_motion_fig} \caption{ {Schematic illustrations of the correspondence between the configurations of the boson cluster with }$N=3${\ and the basis states for the effective Hamiltonian given in Eq.\ \protect\ref{H_eff}. (a1-a4) Four configurations of the boson cluster with }$N=3${. These are degenerate states of the Hamiltonian }$H_{0}(\protect\kappa =0)${ . Here, only the hopping processes within a dimer are presented. (b1-b4) The sites in the chain represent the basis states of the effective Hamiltonian. elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig1} \caption{Plots of $p_{l}(t)$ defined in Eq.\ (\protect\ref{p_l}) and $ P_{j}(t)$ defined in Eq.\ (\protect\ref{Pj(t)}), obtained by numerical diagonalizations. Here, (a1) and (a2) correspond to the effective Hamiltonian $H_{\mathrm{eff}}^{[3]}$\ with $\protect\kappa =1.0$ and $1.0i$, respectively. (b1) and (b2) correspond to the Hamiltonian $H_{\mathrm{B}}$\ with $\protect\kappa =1.0$ and $1.0i$, respectively. Other system paraneters are $\protect\omega =0.1$, $U=V=5.0$, and $N=3$. It indicates that $H_{ \mathrm{eff}}^{[3]}$\ and $H_{\mathrm{B}}$ have the similar dynamics, demonstrating a multi-ladder energy level structure.} \label{fig:fig1}

elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{fig3} \caption{Plots of the dressed energy $\protect\varepsilon $\ as a function of $\protect\kappa $, given by Eq. (\protect\ref{dressed energy}) for understanding the energy level structures of the extended Bose-Hubbard model subjected to a linear potential. Several representative energy levels for the systems are selected: (a) $N=3$ and (b) $N=4$. The system parameters are $U=V=5.0$ and $\protect\omega =0.1$. The black dashed lines are added to indicate the relations between the curves. The orange dashed lines are added to indicate the coalescing WSL. We see that the patterns consist of three and four families of curves, respectively.} \label{fig:fig3}
